other_metrics.time_transfer_precision: 0.30 ns level for BDS-3 PPP using PPP-B2a

grounding_quotes: The time transfer based on BDS-3 PPP using the PPP-B2a is feasible and achieves a 0.30 ns level.
the reliability of time transfer using the PPP-B2b outperforms that of PPP using CNES products due to the problem of unstable internet communication.

main_results.summary: BDS-3 PPP time transfer with PPP-B2b is feasible (0.30 ns level) and more reliable than CNES real-time products, but GPS-only PPP-B2b is not recommended and BDS-3/GPS combination brings no significant improvement.
key_findings: GPS PPP with PPP-B2b is not recommended.
BDS-3 PPP using PPP-B2b (referred to as PPP-B2a) achieves 0.30 ns time transfer precision.
Reliability of PPP-B2b time transfer is better than that using CNES products.
BDS-3/GPS combined PPP time transfer precision is not significantly improved over BDS-3-only.
grounding_quotes: the GPS PPP with the PPP-B2b is not recommended.

compared to BDS-3-only, the precision of time transfer based on BDS-3/GPS PPP was not improved significantly.

research_problem.problem_statement: Evaluate the performance of PPP time transfer using BDS-3 PPP-B2b corrections for single-system and combined GPS/BDS-3 solutions.
research_questions: How does PPP time transfer with PPP-B2b perform for BDS-3, GPS, and BDS-3/GPS?

technical_route.overall_flow: 1. Recover precise orbit and clocks from broadcast ephemeris and PPP-B2b corrections. 2. Present PPP time transfer method. 3. Assess quality of real-time products. 4. Describe experimental dataset and processing strategies. 5. Validate PPP time transfer with PPP-B2b.
key_steps: Combine broadcast ephemeris with PPP-B2b orbit/clock corrections
Apply OSB corrections to pseudorange observations
Formulate dual-frequency IF observation equations
Estimate receiver clock offset for time transfer
grounding_quotes: The method of recovery of precise clock offset and orbit will be presented first, followed by PPP time transfer with the PPP-B2b.

The quality of real-time products will then be assessed and analyzed. Next, the experimental dataset and the processing strategies are described in detail. Following that is the investigation and validation of PPP time transfer with the PPP-B2b correction.

technical_route.overall_flow: 1) Receive BDS-3/GPS observations and decode PPP-B2b corrections from GEO satellites. 2) Recover precise orbit and clock. 3) Apply OSB and IF PPP model to estimate receiver clock offset (BDT-local time). 4) Compute time transfer between stations via clock differences.

Recover precise orbit and clock using broadcast ephemeris and PPP-B2b corrections
Apply OSB correction to pseudorange
Perform ionosphere-free PPP to estimate receiver clock offset
Calculate time difference between BDT and local time, then between stations
grounding_quotes: For each station, receiving the BDS-3 or GPS pseudorange and phase observation and 3 GEO satellite signals for decoding PPP-B2b corrections.
Based on BDS-3 CNAV1 and GPS LNAV broadcast ephemeris and decoded the PPP-B2b correction, precise orbit and clock offset are then recovered.

method.method_name: PPP-based time transfer with BDS-3 PPP-B2b corrections
method.description: Standard ionosphere-free PPP model is used to estimate receiver clock offset relative to BDT. Time transfer is achieved by differencing clock offsets between two stations.
method.algorithm_details: Ionosphere-free combination of dual-frequency pseudorange and phase, with elevation-dependent weighting incorporating satellite system error factor and URA from PPP-B2b. OSB corrections applied. No explicit Kalman filter details given.
method.baseline_method: Comparison with CNES real-time products and WUM final products.

formulas.description: Stochastic model: elevation-dependent weighting with URA from PPP-B2b
Receiver clock offset expressed as difference between local time and BDT
Time transfer between two stations as difference of local times

experiments.description: Real-time PPP time transfer experiment using BDS-3 PPP-B2b service
experiments.setup: Two MGEX stations USUD and CUSV in Asia-Pacific; USUD equipped with an H-maser atomic clock.
experiments.conditions: PPP-B2b real-time corrections compared with CNES real-time products; dual-constellation GPS and BDS-3.
grounding_quotes: Since there are not many GNSS stations connected to an atomic clock in the Asia–Pacific region for time transfer, we only chose 2 GNSS stations, namely USUD and CUSV (Fig. 5). USUD is equipped with an H-master clock.

datasets.access_info: MGEX stations (no specific download link provided)
grounding_quotes: we only chose 2 GNSS stations, namely USUD and CUSV
The mean RMS values in the RAC vector are about (0.10, 0.43, 0.23) m.
The average STD values of GPS satellite clocks are about 0.13 ns and 0.06 ns for PPP-B2b and CNES real-time products.
The average accuracies of the BDS-3 clock from PPP-B2b and CNES are 0.12 ns and 0.23 ns, respectively.

main_results.summary: PPP-B2b orbit precision for GPS is worse than CNES (especially cross and along), while BDS-3 clocks from PPP-B2b outperform CNES ones, likely due to ISL. The experiment uses two MGEX stations for time transfer.
key_findings: GPS PPP-B2b orbit RMS: (0.10, 0.43, 0.23) m vs CNES (0.02, 0.03, 0.02) m.
BDS-3 PPP-B2b clock STD: 0.12 ns vs CNES 0.23 ns.
limitations: Regional network for GPS in PPP-B2b limits GPS orbit accuracy compared to global CNES network.
grounding_quotes: The precision of GPS orbit in PPP-B2b is obviously worse than that of CNES, especially in the cross and along components.
More interestingly, the precision of the BDS-3 satellite's clock from PPP-B2b outperforms that of BDS-3 satellites from CNES
The reason is that a global network is employed by CNES, while only a regional network is applied for GPS in the PPP-B2b

main_results.summary: GPS and BDS-3 orbit/clock quality from PPP-B2b was assessed against WUM final products. PPP-B2b GPS orbit errors were larger than CNES, but BDS-3 orbit errors were comparable. Convergence times: BDS-3 PPP with B2b ~39.5 min, GPS PPP with B2b ~65.5 min; combined BDS-3/GPS reduced convergence by 16.4% over BDS-3 only. CNES and WUM products achieved shorter convergence. Frequency stability of time transfer at 15,360 s was on the order of 10⁻¹³.

main_results.summary: GPS PPP time transfer using PPP-B2b is not recommended due to lower precision (0.89 ns) and systematic bias. BDS-3 PPP time transfer using PPP-B2b achieves 0.30 ns precision, better than CNES (0.35 ns) but worse than WUM (0.14 ns). BDS-3/GPS PPP slightly improves precision. BDS-3 PPP-B2b provides better reliability without internet-induced reconvergence.
key_findings: GPS PPP-B2b time transfer precision (0.89 ns) is significantly worse than CNES (0.23 ns); not recommended.
BDS-3 PPP-B2b time transfer precision (0.30 ns) outperforms CNES (0.35 ns) and WUU (0.54 ns).
BDS-3 PPP-B2b satellite clock precision (0.12 ns) better than CNES (0.23 ns) due to ISL observations.
BDS-3/GPS combined PPP slightly improves time transfer precision over BDS-3 alone.
Frequency stability of BDS-3, GPS, and BDS-3/GPS PPP around 2.11E-12.
BDS-3 PPP-B2b has better reliability: sometimes does not re-converge due to Internet instability (referring to real-time without internet dependency).
limitations: GPS PPP-B2b time transfer not recommended due to lower precision and systematic bias.
